section.title: The Reign of Joash
section.sectionRationale: As long as Jehoiada the priest lives, Joash faithfully repairs the temple through a nationwide collection. But after Jehoiada's death, Joash abandons the LORD for Asherah poles and idols, and when Jehoiada's own son Zechariah is raised up by the Spirit to rebuke the nation, Joash has him stoned to death in the temple courtyard. Aram then defeats Judah's much larger army, and Joash himself is assassinated on his sickbed by his own servants.
section.thesis: Joash's reign shows that reform inherited from a mentor's influence is not the same as a heart truly given to God, for once Jehoiada is gone Joash abandons the LORD so completely that he murders the very prophet who warned him, and the same measure he showed Zechariah is measured back to him in his own violent end.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Joash repairs the temple under Jehoiada's guidance, verses 1 to 14; Jehoiada's death and burial with the kings, verses 15 to 16; Joash's apostasy and the murder of Zechariah, verses 17 to 22; and Judah's defeat by Aram and Joash's assassination, verses 23 to 27.

units.label: Joash repairs the temple
units.note: he set his heart on repairing

verses.text: Joash was seven years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem forty years. His mother's name was Zibiah; she was from Beersheba.
And Joash did what was right in the eyes of the LORD all the days of Jehoiada the priest.
Jehoiada took for him two wives, and he had sons and daughters.
Some time later, Joash set his {{lev|heart}} on repairing the house of the LORD.

So he gathered the priests and Levites and said, "Go out to the cities of Judah and collect the money due annually from all Israel, to repair the house of your God. Do it quickly." The Levites, however, did not make haste.

So the king called Jehoiada the high priest and said, "Why have you not required the Levites to bring from Judah and Jerusalem the tax imposed by Moses the servant of the LORD and by the assembly of Israel for the Tent of the Testimony?"
For the sons of that wicked woman Athaliah had broken into the house of God and had even used the sacred objects of the house of the LORD for the Baals.
At the king's command a chest was made and placed outside, at the gate of the house of the LORD.
And a proclamation was issued in Judah and Jerusalem that they were to bring to the LORD the tax imposed by Moses the servant of God on Israel in the wilderness.
All the officers and all the people rejoiced and brought their contributions, and they dropped them in the chest until it was full.

Whenever the chest was brought by the Levites to the king's overseers and they saw that there was a large amount of money, the royal scribe and the officer of the high priest would come and empty the chest and carry it back to its place. They did this daily and gathered the money in abundance.

Then the king and Jehoiada would give the money to those who supervised the labor on the house of the LORD to hire stonecutters and carpenters to restore the house of the LORD, as well as workers in iron and bronze to repair the house of the LORD.
So the workmen labored, and in their hands the repair work progressed. They restored the house of God according to its specifications, and they {{chazaq|reinforced}} it.

When they were finished, they brought the rest of the money to the king and Jehoiada, and with it were made articles for the house of the LORD, utensils for the service and for the burnt offerings, dishes, and other objects of gold and silver. Throughout the days of Jehoiada, burnt offerings were presented regularly in the house of the LORD.
When Jehoiada was old and full of years, he died at the age of 130.
And Jehoiada was buried with the kings in the City of David, because he had done what was good in Israel for God and His temple.
After the death of Jehoiada, however, the officials of Judah came and paid homage to the king, and he listened to them.
They abandoned the house of the LORD, the God of their fathers, and served the Asherah poles and idols. So wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem for this guilt of theirs.
Nevertheless, the LORD sent prophets to bring the people back to Him and to testify against them, but they would not listen.

Then the {{ruach|Spirit}} of God came upon Zechariah son of Jehoiada the priest, who stood up before the people and said to them, "This is what God says: 'Why do you transgress the commandments of the LORD so that you cannot prosper? Because you have forsaken the LORD, He has forsaken you.'"
But they conspired against Zechariah, and by order of the king, they stoned him in the courtyard of the house of the LORD.

Thus King Joash failed to {{zakar|remember}} the kindness that Zechariah's father Jehoiada had extended to him. Instead, Joash killed Jehoiada's son. As he lay dying, Zechariah said, "May the LORD see this and call you to account."

units.label: The death of Joash
units.note: his own servants conspired against him

verses.text: In the spring, the army of Aram went to war against Joash. They entered Judah and Jerusalem and destroyed all the leaders of the people, and they sent all the plunder to their king in Damascus.

Although the Aramean army had come with only a few men, the LORD delivered into their hand a very great army. Because Judah had forsaken the LORD, the God of their fathers, judgment was executed on Joash.

And when the Arameans had withdrawn, they left Joash severely wounded. His own servants conspired against him for shedding the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest, and they killed him on his bed. So he died and was buried in the City of David, but not in the tombs of the kings.
Those who conspired against Joash were Zabad son of Shimeath the Ammonitess and Jehozabad son of Shimrith the Moabitess.

The accounts of the sons of Joash and the many pronouncements about him, and of the restoration of the house of God, are indeed written in the Treatise of the Book of the Kings. And his son Amaziah reigned in his place.

lev.gloss: Heart. Joash sets his lev on repairing the temple, showing genuine early devotion; the tragedy of his reign is that the heart set on God's house under Jehoiada's influence does not remain fixed on God once that influence is gone.

chazaq.gloss: To strengthen, repair, or reinforce. The workmen chazaq, reinforce, the temple structurally; the same root elsewhere means to be strong or courageous, a wordplay the chapter's tragedy underlines, since Joash reinforces a building while his own heart grows weak toward God.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, or breath. The ruach of God comes upon Zechariah, empowering him to speak God's own indictment; that the king murders the very prophet raised up by God's own Spirit shows the full depth of Joash's apostasy.

zakar.gloss: To remember. Joash failed to zakar, remember, the kindness Jehoiada had shown him; the same root elsewhere describes God remembering His covenant, making Joash's forgetfulness of a human benefactor's kindness a bitter contrast to the LORD's own faithful remembering of His promises.

basic.context: Joash reigns well for many years, faithfully organizing a nationwide collection to repair the temple, as long as his mentor Jehoiada the priest is alive to guide him. But once Jehoiada dies, the officials of Judah persuade Joash to abandon the LORD's temple for Asherah poles and idols. When Jehoiada's own son Zechariah, moved by God's Spirit, publicly rebukes the nation's unfaithfulness, Joash orders him stoned to death in the very temple courtyard his father had served. Aram then defeats Judah's much larger army, wounding Joash, and his own servants finish the job by assassinating him on his sickbed, in direct retribution for Zechariah's blood. He is buried, like Jehoram before him, outside the tombs of the kings.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative tracing the tragic arc of a king whose reform depended on someone else's godliness rather than his own settled devotion to the LORD. It is not a claim that mentorship or good influence is worthless, but a sober warning that reform inherited from another person's faithfulness must become one's own conviction, or it will collapse the moment that influence is removed.
basic.originalLanguages: Joash sets his lev, heart, on repairing the temple, real devotion for a time. The workmen chazaq, reinforce, the building itself even as the king's own resolve weakens once Jehoiada is gone. When the ruach, Spirit, of God empowers Zechariah to speak a final warning, Joash's response, having the very son of his benefactor stoned, exposes just how completely he had forsaken the LORD.
basic.typology: Zechariah, the righteous son of a faithful priest, killed in the temple courtyard by the very king he served and warned, stands among the innocent blood the Old Testament remembers, and Jesus Himself names Zechariah's murder alongside Abel's as bookending the violence done to God's righteous messengers, pointing forward to His own unjust death at the hands of those He came to save.
basic.christ: Jesus explicitly names Zechariah son of Berechiah, likely this same Zechariah or closely connected to him, alongside Abel as marking the full measure of righteous blood shed in Israel's history, Matthew 23:35, showing that this murder in the temple courtyard is remembered by Christ Himself as a paradigm of how God's people can kill their own faithful witnesses. Zechariah's dying plea, may the LORD see this and call you to account, anticipates the far greater contrast of Christ's own dying words, Father, forgive them, showing the surpassing mercy of the cross beyond even righteous demand for justice.
basic.god: God is patient, sending prophet after prophet to call His wandering people back before raising up Zechariah with a final, Spirit empowered word. Yet God is also just; He remembers Zechariah's innocent blood and brings Joash to account through the very army he might have defeated and the very servants who should have protected him, showing that forsaking the LORD carries consequences that eventually catch up even a once faithful king.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 12 gives the parallel account of Joash's temple repairs. Matthew 23:35 names the blood of Zechariah son of Berechiah as part of the righteous blood required of that generation. Luke 23:34 contrasts Zechariah's dying plea for justice with Christ's dying plea for mercy, Father, forgive them. Galatians 6:7 states plainly that whatever a man sows he will also reap, fulfilled exactly in Joash's own violent end.
basic.oneStory: Joash's reign, faithful while his mentor lived and then catastrophically apostate once that influence was removed, culminating in the murder of a Spirit filled prophet and Joash's own violent death, warns every reader that borrowed faith is not saving faith. This tragedy of righteous blood shed in the temple courts anticipates the far greater righteous blood shed at Calvary, where the dying plea shifts from a cry for justice to a cry of forgiveness that reaches even those who shed it.

context.literary: The chapter is carefully structured around Jehoiada's life span: faithful reform while he lives, sudden apostasy immediately upon his death, and a final judgment narrative that mirrors, almost point for point, the earlier account of Jehoram's shameful, unmourned death, reinforcing the chronicler's pattern that unfaithfulness reaps a matching harvest.
context.historical: The temple repair required a large scale, sustained fundraising effort collected in a public chest, reflecting Israel's ancient wilderness tax for the tabernacle, Exodus 30:11-16, now applied to Solomon's temple generations later; such infrastructure projects depended heavily on royal initiative and priestly oversight working in tandem.
context.canonical: Jesus' own reference to Zechariah's blood in Matthew 23:35 shows this account was remembered by later generations as a defining instance of Israel's rejection of God's prophetic messengers, a pattern climaxing in the rejection of Christ Himself, the greatest of all the prophets.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To trace how Joash's faithfulness, dependent on Jehoiada's living influence rather than his own settled devotion, collapsed entirely after the priest's death, climaxing in the murder of Jehoiada's own son and God's just judgment on Joash through military defeat and assassination.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical decline and its consequences; it does not claim every mentor's death produces such apostasy in a protege. What endures is the warning that faith borrowed from another's influence must become genuinely one's own, and the pattern that forsaking the LORD, however delayed, brings real consequence.

debates.question: Was Joash ever genuinely faithful, or was his early reign merely political conformity to Jehoiada's power?

views.label: Genuine, if dependent, early faithfulness
views.case: Verse 2 states plainly that Joash did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, and his own initiative in verse 4, setting his heart on repairing the temple, and his rebuke of the Levites' slowness in verse 6, suggest real personal engagement, not mere passive conformity.

views.label: Faithfulness contingent entirely on external influence
views.case: The qualifying phrase all the days of Jehoiada the priest, verse 2, and the totality of his collapse the moment Jehoiada dies, suggest his righteousness was never deeply rooted in his own heart but was sustained entirely by his mentor's presence and authority.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter's central warning stands: whatever the depth of Joash's early sincerity, it did not survive the removal of external influence, illustrating the difference between reform imposed or modeled by others and conviction that endures on its own.

study.originalLanguages: Joash genuinely sets his lev, heart, on the temple's repair, and the workers chazaq, reinforce, its structure even as the king's own heart weakens once Jehoiada is gone. When the ruach, Spirit, of God raises up Zechariah with a final warning, Joash's murderous response reveals a heart that had, in the end, utterly forsaken the LORD who had once been dear to him.

typology.type: The righteous prophet's blood shed in the temple courtyard by the very king he served
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own unjust death at the hands of those He came to save, remembered by Jesus alongside Zechariah's blood as the full measure of Israel's rejection of God's messengers, Matthew 23:35.

typology.type: Zechariah's dying plea, may the LORD see this and call you to account
typology.fulfillment: Answered and surpassed by Christ's dying prayer, Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do, Luke 23:34.

study.christ: Jesus names the blood of Zechariah alongside that of Abel as marking the full measure of righteous blood shed throughout Israel's history, Matthew 23:35, showing that this murder in the temple courtyard stood in the church's memory as a paradigm case of God's people killing God's own messengers, a pattern that reaches its climax in the crucifixion of Christ Himself, the greatest of all the prophets, in the very city where Zechariah died. Yet where Zechariah rightly cried for God to see and call his murderer to account, Christ's dying words move beyond justice to mercy, Father, forgive them, opening a door of forgiveness even for those who shed His blood.
study.god: God's patience is remarkable, sending prophet after prophet before raising up Zechariah with one final, Spirit empowered appeal. Yet His justice is equally certain; He remembers innocent blood and brings a once faithful king to account through military defeat and assassination, showing that forsaking the LORD, even after years of apparent devotion, still brings a real and personal reckoning.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of Joash's temple repairs and his eventual assassination, giving additional detail on his reign.
Jesus names the blood of Zechariah, likely this same or a closely related Zechariah, as part of the righteous blood required of His generation.
Christ's dying prayer of forgiveness stands in stark and gracious contrast to Zechariah's dying plea for justice.
Whatever a man sows he will also reap, the principle vividly fulfilled in Joash's own violent end.

study.oneStory: Joash's reign, faithful while dependent on Jehoiada's influence and catastrophically apostate the moment that influence was gone, warns that reform must take root in one's own heart or it will not survive. The righteous blood of Zechariah, shed in the temple courts by the very king he had every reason to trust, anticipates the far greater injustice done to Christ, whose own dying words transform a demand for justice into an offer of forgiveness for all who will receive it.

quiz.q: How long did Joash do what was right in the eyes of the LORD?

options.text: All the days of Jehoiada the priest, suggesting his faithfulness depended heavily on Jehoiada's living influence.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this exact qualifying phrase, which the rest of the chapter shows to be significant.

options.text: For his entire forty year reign without exception.
options.note: The text explicitly qualifies his righteousness to the days of Jehoiada, and he apostatizes completely after the priest's death.

options.text: Only for the first seven years of his life before becoming king.
options.note: The qualifying phrase concerns the days of Jehoiada's life, not merely Joash's childhood before his reign began.

options.text: The text gives no indication of how long his righteousness lasted.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly qualifies his righteous conduct to the days of Jehoiada the priest.

quiz.q: How was the temple repair actually funded?

options.text: Through a nationwide collection using a chest placed at the temple gate, filled by the willing contributions of the people.
options.note: Verses 8 to 11 describe this collection method and the people's generous response.

options.text: Entirely from the king's personal treasury with no public involvement.
options.note: The text describes a public, nationwide collection effort, not funding solely from the king's private resources.

options.text: By melting down existing temple furnishings.
options.note: New articles were made once repairs were finished, but the funding itself came through the collected tax, not melted furnishings.

options.text: No method of funding the repairs is described.
options.note: Verses 5 to 11 give a detailed account of the collection method.

quiz.q: What happened after the death of Jehoiada the priest?

options.text: The officials of Judah persuaded Joash to abandon the temple and serve Asherah poles and idols instead.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record this sudden and complete apostasy immediately following Jehoiada's death.

options.text: Joash continued exactly as before, with no change in his devotion.
options.note: The text explicitly records a dramatic reversal into idolatry, not a continuation of his prior faithfulness.

options.text: Joash appointed a new high priest to continue Jehoiada's reforms.
options.note: Rather than continuing reform, the text records Joash's abandonment of the temple for idolatry.

options.text: No change in Judah's worship is recorded after Jehoiada's death.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 explicitly record a dramatic change toward idolatry.

quiz.q: How did Joash respond to Zechariah's Spirit empowered rebuke?

options.text: He ordered Zechariah stoned to death in the courtyard of the house of the LORD, forgetting the kindness of Zechariah's father Jehoiada.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 record this murderous response and its stark contrast to Jehoiada's earlier kindness to Joash.

options.text: He repented immediately and restored true worship.
options.note: The opposite occurred; Joash had Zechariah killed rather than repenting at his warning.

options.text: He exiled Zechariah from Jerusalem rather than killing him.
options.note: The text specifically records Zechariah's execution by stoning, not exile.

options.text: He ignored Zechariah's warning with no further action.
options.note: Verse 21 records the specific, violent action taken against Zechariah.

options.text: Aram defeated Judah's larger army and wounded him, then his own servants assassinated him on his bed in retribution for Zechariah's blood.
options.note: Verses 23 to 25 record this exact sequence of military defeat and assassination.

options.text: He died peacefully of old age after a long and secure reign.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a violent military defeat followed by assassination, not a peaceful death.

options.text: His son Amaziah overthrew him in a coup.
options.note: The conspirators are named as Zabad and Jehozabad, his own servants, not his son Amaziah, who succeeded him afterward.

options.text: The text does not describe how his reign ended.
options.note: Verses 23 to 25 give a detailed account of his defeat and assassination.

questions: Joash's faithfulness seemed to depend heavily on Jehoiada's living influence rather than his own settled conviction. Whose influence is currently holding your own faith together, and how might God be calling you to make that faith genuinely your own?
The officials of Judah persuaded Joash toward idolatry the moment his mentor was gone. What voices are waiting for a weak moment to pull you away from what you know is right?
Zechariah spoke God's word at great personal cost, and it cost him his life. Where is God calling you to speak an unwelcome truth, even if it may cost you something?
Joash forgot the kindness Jehoiada had shown him and repaid it with violence. Is there someone whose past kindness to you deserves more gratitude and honor than you are currently giving it?

Joash's story ends in the very consequences he brought on Zechariah. Where do you need to trust that God sees injustice, even when it goes unaddressed for a season, and will call every account to settle in His own time?

translationNotes.note: The tax imposed by Moses refers to the half shekel wilderness census tax of Exodus 30:11-16, here reapplied as an ongoing temple maintenance collection under Joash.

This Zechariah, son of Jehoiada the priest, is distinct from the minor prophet Zechariah of the postexilic period; Jesus' reference in Matthew 23:35 to Zechariah son of Berechiah has occasioned some discussion, since this Zechariah is called son of Jehoiada, though many understand the names to refer to the same historical figure or a close relation within the priestly family.

For shedding the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest follows the Septuagint and Vulgate reading; most Hebrew manuscripts read of the sons, plural, though the singular fits the immediate narrative concerning Zechariah specifically.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 24:1-27. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
